title: OPEC+ Floods the Market as Gulf Producers Race for Share, Russia Burns
subhead: OPEC+ approves a fifth straight monthly output hike, the UAE pumps near-record volumes after quitting the cartel, and Saudi Arabia slashes prices by the most in two decades — all while Ukraine's drone campaign creates a nationwide fuel crisis in Russia and Japan sounds the alarm over China's rare earth chokehold.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Adds 188K BPD for August as Oil Surplus Fears Mount
sections.body:
The seven OPEC+ countries with voluntary production cuts — Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iraq, Kuwait, Kazakhstan, Algeria, and Oman — agreed on July 5 to raise output targets by 188,000 barrels per day starting in August, the fifth consecutive monthly increase. Under the new quotas, Saudi Arabia's required production rises to 10.416 million bpd and Russia's to 9.887 million bpd, according to OPEC's official statement.

The increases have been largely theoretical until now: the Strait of Hormuz closure during the Iran war kept actual output well below quota for the Gulf producers. But with tanker traffic recovering since the June 17 US-Iran ceasefire, these paper increases are beginning to translate into real barrels. Analysts at XS.com warn that global stockpiles may struggle to absorb the surge if the Strait stays open and flows remain uninterrupted.

Brent crude closed Monday at $71.99/barrel, down 14 cents on the day and nearly 40% below its March peak above $126. WTI settled at $68.55. The Brent futures curve tipped into contango last week for the first time this year — the six-month spread near minus 56 cents — signaling the market is paying for storage rather than scarcity. OPEC's own monthly report has trimmed 2026 demand growth in back-to-back months to under 1 million bpd.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is accelerating output into a softening demand environment, betting that market share matters more than price support. With the UAE now outside the quota system and pumping freely, the cartel's cohesion is under strain — and the oversupply signal is flashing.

sections.heading: UAE Pumps Near-Record Volumes After OPEC Exit, Sparking Price War Signals
sections.body:
The UAE raised crude output to above 3.8 million bpd in June, its highest level in over six years, after formally withdrawing from OPEC and OPEC+ on May 1. Energy Minister Suhail Al Mazrouei said the country's tens of billions in upstream investments — expanding capacity to 5 million bpd — meant it needed to maximize returns rather than keep production offline.

ADNOC has shifted the official selling prices for its offshore Upper Zakum, Das, and Umm Lulu grades from a Murban-linked formula to the Dubai benchmark, aligning those medium-sour streams with competing regional crude. The company has also been offering discounted cargoes through tenders to expand its customer base. Meanwhile, Saudi Aramco set the official selling price for its flagship Arab Light crude to Asia at $1.50/bbl below the Oman/Dubai average for August — the biggest monthly cut since Reuters records began in 2003.

Mizuho's Robert Yawger said the moves increasingly look like Gulf producers are gearing up for a price war. The UAE's unrestricted pumping and Saudi's aggressive price cuts are a one-two punch that could crater prices further if demand doesn't keep pace.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE's OPEC exit has removed the last restraint on Abu Dhabi's production ambitions. Combined with Saudi price cuts, this signals a shift from coordinated supply management to competitive market-share grabbing — a dynamic that could push Brent into the $60s or lower.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Drone Campaign Triggers Russia-Wide Fuel Crisis
sections.body:
Ukraine struck three Russian oil refineries and an oil terminal at the Baltic Sea port of Vysotsk on July 6, according to Ukraine's military and security service. Drone debris also damaged the ports of Ust-Luga — a major oil export outlet — and caused a power blackout in Sevastopol, Crimea. The strikes hit the Omsk refinery, Russia's largest, as well as facilities in Yaroslavl and Leningrad regions.

These attacks are the latest escalation in a sustained campaign that has pushed Russia into a nationwide fuel crisis. According to CNN analysis, nearly all of Russia's 83 regions are experiencing gasoline shortages or supply disruptions. The Norsi refinery, Russia's fourth-largest, stopped selling petrol and diesel on July 2 after a major unit was destroyed — the fifth Russian refinery to shut down. Repairs could take up to a year.

Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak acknowledged the crisis, saying the country has enough fuel for domestic demand but that panic buying has increased consumption by 20-30%. Russia has slashed fuel quality standards and may suspend diesel exports. In Irkutsk, 5,000 km from Ukraine, authorities installed portable toilets at fuel stations because people were waiting for hours. One woman told the New York Times she waited 18 hours in a fuel queue with her 18-month-old child.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone campaign has achieved what conventional military pressure couldn't: a genuine domestic energy crisis inside one of the world's largest oil producers. The disruption to Russia's refining capacity — and the potential suspension of diesel exports — could tighten global middle distillate markets even as crude oversupplies.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Recovers, UK-France Push Multinational Naval Mission
sections.body:
Japanese-owned supertankers carrying Saudi crude were heading through the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, joining a fleet of vessels resuming transit after the US-Iran ceasefire. According to Reuters, another two Japanese-owned supertankers were en route to exit the Gulf, signaling that the waterway is gradually returning to normal operations.

But the recovery is not instant. Mine-clearing operations could delay full shipping normalization by 40-50 days, according to estimates cited in energy industry reports. The UK and France are pushing to launch a multinational naval mission to secure the Strait within days, with defense sources saying the force will remove Iranian mines and escort ships through the waterway.

Trump said the US 'won't need much help' reopening Hormuz, but France and Britain are proceeding with their own multinational effort regardless. The two-track approach — US-Iran deal on one side, European-led maritime security on the other — reflects lingering distrust about the durability of the ceasefire. Shipping groups Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd announced they will resume some sailings through the Suez Canal, shortening Asia-Europe transit times by about four weeks.
sections.whyItMatters: Hormuz is reopening, but the mine-clearing timeline means full normalization is weeks away. The UK-France push for an independent naval mission signals that European powers want insurance beyond the US-Iran deal — and are positioning for long-term Gulf security involvement regardless of what Washington and Tehran agree.

sections.heading: Japan's Rare Earth Crisis Deepens as China Keeps the Spigot Closed
sections.body:
Corporate Japan is sounding alarms over rare earth shortages after China's export controls caused shipments to Japan to plummet more than 80% year-on-year in March and April 2026. Nomura Research Institute estimates the damage could reach $4.3 billion in lost Japanese production over three months, ballooning to $17 billion if restrictions persist for a full year.

Japan currently sources roughly 60-70% of its rare earth imports from China — already an improvement from nearly 90% dependency in 2010. But for heavy rare earths like dysprosium and terbium, which are critical for high-performance magnets in EV motors and wind turbines, Japan's reliance on China remains close to 100%.

China imposed the export controls on January 6, 2026, specifically targeting Japanese entities and military-related applications — a move widely interpreted as retaliation for PM Takaichi's comments linking Japan's national security to potential Taiwan-related conflicts. At the G7 summit, Takaichi pushed a mineral stockpiling plan, seeking to bridge divides on critical mineral supply chain resilience. Meanwhile, the rare earth export controls China expanded in October 2025 continue to ripple through global markets eight months later.
sections.whyItMatters: China is weaponizing rare earth supply with surgical precision against Japan — the world's most exposed major economy. The 80% shipment collapse isn't just a trade dispute; it's a live demonstration of how critical mineral dependency can be leveraged in a geopolitical confrontation, and Japan's $17B exposure shows how costly it is to diversify away from a dominant supplier.

sections.heading: G7 Sanctions Target Russian Shadow Fleet and Energy Revenues
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in France, multiple leaders announced new sanctions targeting Russia's energy war chest. Canada imposed sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet and energy revenues, PM Mark Carney raising the issue alongside China EV tariffs in his meeting with Trump. The EU adopted a new sanctions package targeting Putin's confessor and Russia's prosecutor general, along with broader measures on energy revenues and propaganda.

The UK announced new sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet and seized a vessel in the English Channel. British Royal Marines seized a Russian shadow fleet oil tanker. Separately, Ukraine struck two shadow fleet tankers — the Gambian-flagged Kairos and Virat — in the Sea of Azov using Sea Baby naval drones. Both vessels were empty, heading to Novorossiysk to pick up cargo. The Kairos caught fire with critical damage; the Virat suffered minor damage.

Ukraine's strike exposed the financial architecture behind the shadow fleet: crew members are reportedly paid in Bitcoin and stablecoins, revealing an extensive crypto-based sanctions evasion network. The combination of Western naval interdiction and Ukrainian military action is squeezing the shadow fleet from both ends — though Russia has built redundancy into the system.
sections.whyItMatters: The shadow fleet is being attacked on two fronts — Western law enforcement at sea and Ukrainian military drones in port. The crypto payment revelation shows how deep the evasion architecture runs. But Russia's continued ability to move oil through these channels means sanctions impact is still more costly friction than effective blockade.

sections.heading: LNG Trade Hits Record but Middle East Fallout Clouds Outlook
sections.body:
The International Gas Union reported that global LNG trade hit a record in 2025, with strong US exports and rising European imports offsetting weaker Asian purchases. But the Middle East conflict continues to cloud the outlook. European aviation fuel supply has tightened to 80% of normal as Iran war disruptions cut imports, and the full recovery of energy trade flows remains contingent on whether the US-Iran ceasefire holds.

The world has absorbed the loss of over a billion barrels of oil supply since the Iran war began with surprising ease, Reuters reported, but buffer reserves are now drained and long-term peace remains elusive. Hedge funds have cut their net long position in Brent futures to near historical lows, according to Saxo Bank — a signal that speculative money has largely exited the market.

Trump also pushed a critical minerals pricing plan at the G7, but faced skeptical allies and a divided industry. The plan's details remain murky, but it reflects Washington's growing concern about China's dominance in mineral supply chains even as the administration holds off on blacklisting over 100 flagged Chinese firms including DeepSeek, reportedly to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing.
sections.whyItMatters: The LNG record shows the energy system adapted remarkably well to the Iran war shock — but the adaptation came at the cost of drawn-down reserves. With strategic petroleum reserves depleted and hedge fund positioning near historical lows, the system has less cushion for the next disruption. The G7's struggle to align on critical minerals underscores how far the West is from a coordinated response to China's supply chain leverage.

bottomLine: The oil market's war premium has evaporated — Brent at $72, contango returning, hedge funds near record-low longs — but the structural picture is messier than the price suggests. OPEC+ is accelerating output into softening demand, the UAE is pumping unconstrained after quitting the cartel, and Saudi Arabia is slashing prices by the most in two decades, all signals of an emerging price war for market share. Meanwhile, Ukraine's drone campaign has created a genuine domestic fuel emergency in Russia, China is demonstrating rare earth weaponization against Japan with 80% shipment cuts, and global strategic reserves are depleted from absorbing over a billion barrels of Iran war supply losses. The ceasefire calmed Hormuz, but the energy system enters the post-war period with thinner buffers, fractured producer coordination, and new precedents for commodity coercion.
sources: Rigzone, CNBC, Reuters, OilPrice.com, CryptoBriefing, News24, CNN, FXStreet, Financial Express, Saxo Bank analysis, Samaa TV, Seeking Alpha, International Gas Union, GivTrade, XS.com, Mizuho, Nomura Research Institute
